coredata.dc:title: TiO2/BiOI/CQDs: Enhanced photocatalytic properties under visible-light irradiation

coredata.dc:description: In this study, TiO2/CQDs(TC), TiO2/BiOI(TB), TiO2/BiOI/CQDs(TBC) were prepared via a step-by-step method. Photocatalytic properties of the samples were carried out under visible-light irradiation using methyl orange (MO) as the target molecular. Among the samples, TBC showed the best photocatalytic performance, and the photocatalytic efficiency of TBC was more than 2 times higher than that of TiO2, TC and TB samples, respectively. The reasons for the improved photocatalytic performance were discussed in this paper.

CQDs
Photocatalytic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for different samples: (a) TiO2; (b) TC; (c) TB; (d) TBC.
(a) XPS scanning spectrum of the samples of TC, TB, TBC; (b)–(f) High-resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C 1s, (c) Bi 4f, (d) I 3d, (e) O 1s and (f) Ti 2p in the samples.
SEM images of: (a) TC; (b) TB; (c) TBC; (d) TEM images of CQDs.
(a) SEM image of TBC; (b)–(d) TEM image of TBC at different magnification.
Nitrogen absorption–desorption isotherms of (a) TC, (b) TB and (c) TBC.
(a) The UV–vis DRS spectrum of TiO2, TC, TB, TBC; (b)Photocatalytic performance of the samples for the degradation of MO solution; (c) Absorption spectrum of MO in the presence of TBC.
Schematic illustration of the band structure related photocatalytic mechanism of TBC.
The SBET of TiO2, TB and TBC samples.

head.ce:title: TiO2/BiOI/CQDs: Enhanced photocatalytic properties under visible-light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Zhengzhou Road 53, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, TiO2/CQDs(TC), TiO2/BiOI(TB), TiO2/BiOI/CQDs(TBC) were prepared via a step-by-step method. Photocatalytic properties of the samples were carried out under visible-light irradiation using methyl orange (MO) as the target molecular. Among the samples, TBC showed the best photocatalytic performance, and the photocatalytic efficiency of TBC was more than 2 times higher than that of TiO2, TC and TB samples, respectively. The reasons for the improved photocatalytic performance were discussed in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: As known, water pollution issues are posing an increasing attention with the improvement of life standard. Photocatalysis has attracted much interest for researchers, because it has been considered to be a promising technology to deal with this problem. Among many photocatalysts, TiO2 with the advantages of low price, non-toxicity, chemical stability attracts extensive attention [1,2]. However, the wide band gap (3.2eV) of TiO2 limits the utilization of visible-light while just a small fraction (3–5%) of solar energy is available. Meanwhile, the high recombination rate between photogenerated electrons and holes also leads to a decreased photocatalytic activity [3].

For improving the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) has been applied to couple with it [4–6]. BiOI, because of the narrow band gap (1.77–1.92eV), performs outstanding photocatalytic properties under visible-light [5,6]. Up to now, there have been some reports about BiOI used as excellent visible-light photocatalysts. For example, Young et al. reported that BiOI showed the higher photocatalytic activity for RhB and MB, compared with BiOBr and BiOCl [7]. In addition, it has been proven that BiOI could promote separation and transfer of photo-induced carriers as the heterostructure semiconductor, and further enhance the photocatalytic performance [8].

Carbon materials, including carbon fiber, carbon nanotube, carbon quantum dot and graphene, are also extensively studied due to its chemical inertness, nontoxicity, low photobleaching, and excellent biocompatibility [9–14]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), because of their excellent electron transfer ability, have been applied in photocatalysis field and some CQDs/semiconductor systems, such as CQDs/Ag/Ag2O [15], CQDs/BiOBr [16], ZnS/CQDs [17], g-C3N4/CQDs [18], and TiO2/CQDs [19,20] have been prepared. However, up to now, there are few studies about the photocatalytic activity of the ternary TBC composities. So, we introduced BiOI and CQDs to prepare the triple compound substance TBC. In addition, the effects of CQDs and BiOI on TiO2 photocatalyst were also discussed in this paper.

0.8mL KNO3 solution 0.1mol/mL was added into 200mL absolute ethanol. After stirring for 30min, 4.4mL titanium isopropoxide (TTIP) was dropped into the previous solution and continuously stirred until the color of the mixture became milky white. After that, the solution was far from static for 12h. Finally, the precipitate was washed and TiO2 was obtained by annealing at 450°C for 3h.

CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method [21]. For TBC, 0.258g TiO2 and 0.3g CQDs were first dispersed into 10mL EG solution. Then, BiNO3 and KI were dissolved into 7mL and 6mL EG solution, respectively, and the BiNO3 and KI solution were slowly and successively dropped into the above mentioned solution. After stirring for 1h at room temperature, the mixed solution was kept still at 40°C for 3h. Finally, the sample TBC was collected after washing and drying procedure. Samples TC, TB were also prepared using the similar method.

XRD patterns were recorded using an X-Ray diffraction instrument (Rigaku Corporation, Japan, D-MAX2500 diffractometer). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were examined in an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Corporation, UK, XSAM800). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan, JSM-6700F). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) test was observed with a field-emission transmission microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan, JEM-2100). The optical properties were studied in an UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (Varian corporation, USA, Cary500).

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was tested by the degradation of MO solution with a xenon lamp (500 w) as a light source. 0.195g sample was added into 70mL MO solution (5mg/L) and then kept it in the dark with stirring for 30min before irradiation. After the same time intervals, some amount of solution under the visible-light irradiation was taken out and sampled for analysis.

Fig. 1 showed XRD patterns of TiO2, TC, TB and TBC, respectively. For TiO2, four peaks appearing at 25.2, 37.8, 47.8, and 55.1 degrees were assigned to (101), (004), (200) and (211) planes of TiO2 (JCPDS file no. 84-1285) [22], respectively. After decorated by CQDs, there were no obvious peaks of CQDs, which could be attributed to the low amount of CQDs in the TiO2/CQDs sample. For TB, apart from the three peaks of TiO2, the other five peaks at 29.2, 31.3, 46.2, 52.5, and 57.3 degrees belonged to (012), (110), (020), (211) and (112) planes of BiOI (JCPDS file no. 73-2062) [23,24]. From the pattern of TBC, an uplift between 25.2 and 37.5 degrees could be found, while it did not appear in the other diffraction patterns (TiO2, TC, TB) and it could be speculated from CQDs [25].

XPS measurements were carried out to characterize the chemical composition and surface chemical states of the samples, which were shown in Fig. 2(a–f). All binding energies were corrected by using the C1s line at 284.6eV. The full survey spectrum (Fig. 2a) indicated that Bi, I, O, C and Ti elements existed in composite of TBC. The peak for C 1s was still found in TB sample due to the adventitious hydrocarbon from the XPS instrument. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the peaks around 284.7, 286.5 and 288.3eV were associated with C=C, C-O and C=O, respectively [25,26]. Two peaks in the Bi 4f region at 159.4 and 164.7eV belong to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, which suggested that Bi was existed in its normal Bi3+ state (Fig. 2c) [22]. As seen from Fig. 2(d), the peaks located at 618.7 and 630.2eV were found clearly, which could be ascribed to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 [25]. Meanwhile, it confirmed that I was in the form of I- assigned to BiOI. Binding energies obtained from Fig. 2(e) showed those O1s peaks at 529.7 and 531.8eV, which could correspond to Ti-O in TiO2 and -OH on the surface-absorbed water. For TB, the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 2f) displayed a strong peak at 458.5eV, which was in agreement with Ti 2p3/2. Except for TC, a broad bump about 465.1eV was found in the other samples, which due to the overlap for Ti 2p1/2 (464.3eV) and Bi 4d3/2 (467.8eV) according to the previous report [27]. From Fig. 2(c–f), the peaks displayed a slight shift toward the lower binding energy when samples were decorated by CQDs. And these shifts in spectra may attribute to a strong interfacial interaction [28]. Based results above, it could be deduced that BiOI and CQDs coupled with TiO2, successfully.

Fig. 3(a–c) gave the SEM images of TC, TB and TBC. Uniform-sized and well-dispersed TiO2 microspheres could be clearly seen in Fig. 3(a). Fig. 3(b) showed that some nanosheets (BiOI aggregation) attached to the surface of TiO2. From Fig. 3(c), it is interesting to find that nanosheets disappeared when the TiO2 was decorated by BiOI and CQDs at the same time. The phenomenon could be explained as follows: CQDs have abundant surface functional groups and can provide the nucleation sites for the reaction [25], resulting in BiOI nanosheets dispersed and distributed on TiO2 rough surface. From Fig. 3(d), it could be seen that the size of CQDs was about 5–7nm. The interplanar distance was resolved with the spacing of 2.10Å, which well corresponded to the space of (100) plane of CQDs.

In addition, the information about the TBC's morphology and structure was further obtained by SEM and TEM tests. Fig. 4(a) displayed a sphere-like particle with rough surface, and the average diameter was about 350–450nm. As could be seen from Fig. 4(b), it was evident that sample owed a solid spherical structure. As illustrated in magnified HRTEM images of Fig. 4(c), a lamellar structure and some bumps on the surface could be observed, which may correspond to BiOI nanosheets and CQDs, respectively. From Fig. 4(c), the interplanar distance was resolved with the spacings of 3.52Å and 3.00Å, corresponding to the space of (101) plane of TiO2 and (012) plane of BiOI, and the dots marked in red were speculated from CQDs.

Fig. 5 showed the nitrogen adsorption/desorption plot and the adsorption/desorption isotherms could be assigned to type IV. The part of hysteresis loop (0.4 < P/P0 < 1) existed due to the large-pore structure within the crystallites, which suggested that CQDs or BiOI could aggregate on TiO2 [29]. From the specific surface area values ( Table 1), the TBC had the maximum specific surface area (66.1m2/g), while the value for TiO2 and TiO2/BiOI was 48.3m2/g and 47.9m2/g, respectively. As known, the higher surface area was helpful to an enhancement of photocatalytic performance.

The UV–vis absorption spectra for prepared samples were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 6(a). Apparently, for bare TiO2 and TC, the spectra were similar which had less absorbance in the visible-light region. However, for TB and TBC, the absorption edge was further extended to the visible-light at around 420–430nm. Because of the narrow band gap, BiOI enhanced the light absorption in the visible range. It was observed the absorption intensity of sample TBC was not as strong as that of TB, the reason may be that the introduced CQDs covered on the surface of the BiOI, resulting in a decreased light harvest.

The photocatalytic activities of the samples were performed by degrading MO under the visible light, and the degradation curves were presented in Fig. 6(b). As can be seen, the self-degradation of MO can be ignored in the experiments. Compared to the low degradation rate of TiO2 and TC, TB and TBC showed the relatively high rate, which was about 40% and 90%, respectively. It is worth noting that the maximum degradation rate of our sample (TBC) was more than two times compared to P25, which showing the TBC possessed a potential practical application. To further study the degradation process, Fig. 6(c) showed the absorbance spectra of MO degraded by TBC at different time under visible light. The absorbance characteristic peak of MO was clearly found at 450nm, which decreased with time prolonged.

As we all know, the light absorption region, efficiency of charge transportation and separation play important roles in photocatalytic behavior. According to the results above, the presence of CQDs and BiOI facilitates the improvement of photocatalytic activity. Because BiOI can further extend light response region and improve the light harvest in visible-light range, TB exhibits better photocatalytic performance than bare TiO2. When CQDs are introduced, no light absorption extends to the longer wavelength region, TC still displays the improved degradation rate for MO. The phenomenon may be due to the fact that CQDs can act as a medium to accept and transfer electrons, leading to the quick transfer and lower combination of photogenerated charges. Among the samples, TBC nanocomposite showed the most excellent photocatalytic capability, and it can be deduced that the introduced BiOI and CQDs have a synergism effect to improve the degradation rate.

Based upon the discussions, a possible explanation for the better photocatalytic performance on TBC nanocomposite is proposed, as shown in Fig. 7. When photocatalyst is irradiated under visible-light, BiOI could be activated but TiO2 not. Due to the outstanding conductivity of CQDs, the photogenerated electrons from BiOI can transfer to TiO2 quickly, and holes remain in the valence band (VB) of BiOI. As a result, the electron-hole pairs could be separated effectively and thus their combination could be decreased. Besides, the higher specific surface area provides more reaction site during the degradation process. The factors mentioned above could be responsible for the remarkable photocatalytic activity of TBC.

In summary, TBC microspheres were prepared via a step-by-step process. Among all the samples, TBC displayed the most excellent photocatalytic performance in the process of degrading MO under visible-light. According to the analysis and discussion in the paper, morphology, specific surface area, together with band gap structure may charge for the highest photocatalytic activity of TBC. Furthermore, samples modified by BiOI, with a narrow band gap, resulted in an obvious optical response and enhanced activity under the visible-light irradiation. Attached CQDs acted as a center to accept and transport photoelectrons, which facilitated the charges transfer and greatly depressed the recombination of photogenerated charges. Finally, compared with P25, TBC showed a greatly improved photocatalytic activity, thus could be a good candidate in photocatalysis field.
This work was mainly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant nos. 51272115 and 61504073).
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
